materialitySignals: introduces a universal canonical project file
migrates a verified legacy project schema
adds filesystem and IPC trust boundaries
adds cross-host compatibility and distribution obligations

dimensions.definitionOf10: Named users, zero-server and local-file needs, observable web/desktop/cross-host outcomes, recovery expectations, evidence, and consequences of exclusions remain explicit without changing the verified MVP promise.

Web-only, Electron-only, separate-product, Tauri, and shared-core alternatives are compared; accepted scope, deferred capabilities, and north-star trajectory are explicit and reversible where possible.

One host-neutral schema/serializer/core, typed repository/archive/export contracts, exact Electron bridge, opaque path authority, persistence ownership, dependency order, and accepted ADR consequences are executable without architecture invention.

Schema migration, autosave, revisions, snapshots, quota, archive import, desktop fingerprints, partial writes, conflicts, external modification, and stable recovery errors have deterministic state transitions and evidence flows.
evidence: traceability.json migration, recovery, quota, and conflict flows

dimensions.definitionOf10: Correctness, durability, recoverability, security, compatibility, accessibility, storage capacity, performance, offline continuity, release safety, and maintainability are measurable in target-specific scenarios.

Every promise and failure contract maps to shared tests, actual web or packaged Electron UI evidence, cross-host fixtures, clean-machine launch, release checksum, and rehearsed rollback without confusing specified with verified.
evidence: traceability.json work packages and expected behaviors

dimensions.definitionOf10: A competent implementer can execute the dependency-ordered packages without choosing product behavior, data dialects, bridge authority, conflict outcomes, limits, accessibility behavior, or release criteria.
evidence: two independent unfamiliar-implementer review rounds; round 2 PASS

dimensions.definitionOf10: All new views and recovery states have named controls, visible status, safe defaults, keyboard/focus contracts, WCAG 2.2 AA-oriented acceptance, reflow, contrast, target-size, and reduced-motion evidence.
The internal implementer-facing contracts are discoverable, strictly typed, consistently named, fixture-backed, and dependency-ordered; public API/CLI onboarding is correctly excluded.

Assets, untrusted archives/imports, renderer-main privilege crossing, opaque location grants, payload/expansion bounds, strict schemas, navigation controls, no undeclared transfer, mitigations, and adversarial evidence are explicit.

Local durability, recovery, offline continuity, diagnostic error codes, storage status, update lifecycle, clean-machine evidence, and independent host rollback are specified without inventing server monitoring.
Archive, IPC, storage, path, entry, and snapshot limits are numeric; save and maximum-archive workloads have reference environments, p95 or boundary targets, and failure behavior.

Version dimensions, v1-to-v2 transformation, idempotent migration record, source preservation, old/current/future matrix, exact cross-host canonicalization, folder equivalence, and round-trip parity are binding and testable.

Actual Pages subpath/service-worker behavior, deterministic portable artifact naming, SHA-256 publication, unsigned-binary disclosure, clean-machine launch, manual update, independent withdrawal, and data-safe rollback are objective gates.

findings.title: Canonical project evolution was not implementation-grade

evidence.claim: The current schema embeds one character while the update referred broadly to recipes and pack locks without a binding next schema.

findings.problem: An implementer had to invent the canonical project graph and version transition.
findings.impact: Hosts could write incompatible project dialects or lose baseline identity during migration.
findings.remediation: Define project schema version 2, recipe schema version 1, invariants, v1 transformation, and one shared serializer/parser package.
findings.verification: Strict schema fixtures, v1 parity migration, byte-idempotent serializer tests, and cross-host FILE gates.

findings.title: Electron renderer bridge and file authority were underspecified

evidence.claim: The decision required narrow typed preload methods and path validation but did not define methods, channels, result/error types, payload bounds, or authority.

findings.problem: Renderer/main boundaries and filesystem authorization could diverge across implementations.
findings.impact: The renderer could gain excess Node or path authority, and contract tests could not prove the security claim.
findings.remediation: Define frozen SpriteHostBridge methods, internal channel constants, strict request/response schemas, opaque session grants, operation scopes, recents, navigation controls, and payload limits.
findings.verification: Bridge contract tests, invalid-channel/payload/grant tests, packaged renderer-global checks, and navigation adversarial tests.

findings.title: Legacy localStorage migration lacked a state machine and durable marker

evidence.claim: The update promised lossless migration from one legacy key but did not define trigger, transaction, read-back, marker, restart, raw recovery, or failure behavior.

findings.problem: The migration could delete the only legacy copy, repeat, or strand a pending candidate.
findings.impact: Returning verified-MVP users could lose access to work during the persistence upgrade.
findings.remediation: Define pure v1-to-v2 transform, one-transaction candidate/recovery record, strict migration record, startup reconciliation, parity read-back, retention, Retry, and raw recovery download.
findings.verification: Valid, malformed, missing-pack, quota, interrupted, pending-restart, and verified-no-repeat migration fixtures through actual UI.

findings.title: Storage retention, quota thresholds, and archive safety limits were non-numeric

evidence.claim: The contracts required documented bounds and versioned expansion limits but supplied no values.

findings.problem: Quota cleanup and malicious archive handling could not be implemented or tested consistently.
findings.impact: Snapshots could grow without bound and hosts could accept different archive bombs or oversized inputs.
findings.remediation: Set snapshot/age floors, 80/90 percent quota behavior, ordered safe eviction, archive/entry/path/ratio limits, and adversarial fixtures.
findings.verification: Boundary fixtures, quota flow, snapshot policy tests, maximum-archive benchmark, and identical host errors.

findings.title: Conflict and recovery obligations were prose-only

evidence.source: pre-review scenarios, task-flows.md, and compatibility atomicity rules
evidence.claim: Older-tab, quota, snapshot, external-modification, and migration failures appeared as interruptions or gates without complete user flows and exact choices.

findings.problem: Implementers could handle high-risk conflicts differently or omit visible recovery.
findings.impact: Stale tabs or external tools could silently overwrite work, and users could not exercise promised recovery.
findings.remediation: Add first-class migration, snapshot restore, quota recovery, web save-conflict, and desktop external-change scenarios, capabilities, views, controls, flows, and objective behaviors.
findings.verification: All 15 task flows pass through actual UI with no orphan trace records.

findings.title: New surfaces lacked a binding accessibility interaction contract

evidence.claim: Roles and names existed, but keyboard patterns, focus movement/restoration, status announcements, reflow, target size, contrast, and reduced motion were not acceptance obligations.

findings.problem: Accessible names alone could be mistaken for accessible workflows.
findings.impact: Keyboard and assistive-technology users could be blocked in recovery, conflict, import, or close flows.
findings.remediation: Bind WCAG 2.2 AA-oriented semantics, keyboard/focus behavior, safe defaults, announcements, reflow, contrast, target size, and reduced motion to every new flow.
findings.verification: Playwright keyboard/focus/status tests plus automated and manual accessibility checks on supported hosts.

findings.title: Cross-cutting quality and release claims lacked measurable scenarios

evidence.claim: The gate named parity and anomalies but lacked reference workloads, accessibility evidence, artifact naming/checksums, clean-machine fields, and a full data-safe rollback rehearsal.

findings.problem: Release readiness could depend on subjective interpretation or stale local checks.
findings.impact: A host could appear complete while failing at capacity, offline, packaged launch, or rollback boundaries.
findings.remediation: Add QS-001 through QS-012 and strengthen web, Electron, cross-host, performance, artifact, clean-machine, and rollback gates.
findings.verification: Every quality scenario has passing evidence or an approved not-applicable reason before release.

findings.title: Canonical serialization, meaningful edits, and migration/fingerprint metadata still required inference after the first hardening pass

evidence.claim: The reviewer could not determine exact JSON number/string behavior, autosave field classification, migration marker lifecycle, or non-recursive fingerprint structure from the first revision.

findings.problem: Subtle shared-core behaviors remained easy to implement differently even after the main contracts were added.
findings.impact: Byte parity, autosave frequency, restart recovery, or external-change detection could drift by host or module.
findings.remediation: Define strict shared serialization, meaningful/non-meaningful fields, exact migration record/restart rules, explicit non-recursive fingerprint, pack-lock preservation, product-specific web names, leakage fixtures, recents, labels, and dialog defaults.
findings.verification: Independent round 2 confirms all earlier issues resolved and returns PASS with no critical/high findings.

editsMade: Created a reusable product-engineering-spec-review skill and validator.
Expanded traceability from 164 to 203 records and task flows from 11 to 15.

Defined schema version 2, shared canonical serialization, migration, revisions, snapshots, quota, archive limits, conflicts, Electron grants/bridge, error taxonomy, accessibility, offline readiness, and release artifacts.
Aligned architecture decisions and platform contracts and strengthened release and rollback gates.

implementationSequence: Shared schemas, parsers, canonical serializer, migration, error taxonomy, and fixtures
Host-neutral repository, archive, pack, and export contracts
IndexedDB repository and web migration/recovery/conflict UI
GitHub Pages PWA lifecycle and actual-subpath verification
Electron main/preload shell with opaque grants
Desktop folder/archive/export adapters and external-change recovery
Portable release, clean-machine launch, and rollback rehearsal
verificationGates: Product update and traceability validators pass
Shared schema/serializer/migration and adversarial fixtures pass
Every new expected behavior has direct evidence
All 15 task flows pass on their declared actual host
Affected RUN-MVP-001 baseline flows pass on every affected host
QS-001 through QS-012 pass or are explicitly approved not applicable
No unresolved severity 0-2 issue or undeclared anomaly remains

rounds.result: Repaired serializer, edit classification, migration marker, fingerprint clarity, pack locks, browser channel names, strict schemas, recents, conflict labels, and dialog defaults.
All round-1 material issues resolved; no new material product or architecture decision remains. Offline pack wording was clarified as a data-driven low-risk improvement.

stabilization.productVisionAlignment: Preserves one local-first product core, a complete zero-server web baseline, optional desktop file convenience, exact cross-host portability, and a scalable path to the North Star without adding excluded backend or desktop promises.

justifiedChanges.newEvidence: A focused adversarial review demonstrated that the final review record was mutable, generated projections lacked freshness validation, and approved lock changes could cite no repository-resolvable approval artifact.
justifiedChanges.lockedSurface: DEC-UPD001-SPEC-LOCK, spec review metadata, lock manifest controls, generated-artifact validation, and justified-change validation; no customer promise, workflow, schema, host, compatibility, quality threshold, or release behavior changes.
justifiedChanges.noChangeConsequence: A later non-deterministic reviewer could rewrite readiness conclusions or stale generated projections and present self-asserted approval while the source-contract hash remained clean.
options: Keep LOCK-UPD-001-001 unchanged and accept the convergence bypasses
Issue bounded LOCK-UPD-001-002 hardening without product-semantic changes
Add a signed external approval service and cryptographic manifest chain
justifiedChanges.productAlignment: Makes autonomous implementation of the accepted local-first dual-host vision stable without expanding scope or adding backend infrastructure.
justifiedChanges.expectedBenefit: High: closes direct review-churn bypasses and makes generated and review conclusions deterministic inputs to later agents.
justifiedChanges.fullChangeCost: Low: validator and generator checks plus immutable local artifacts; no product implementation, migration, compatibility fixture, actual-UI flow, or release gate is invalidated.
justifiedChanges.blastRadius: Stabilization tooling and review metadata only; the semantic trace changes only the active lock ID and lock-decision evidence.
justifiedChanges.reversibility: Restore the preserved LOCK-UPD-001-001 manifest and prior tooling if the control causes disproportionate friction.
revalidationPlan: Run a fresh adversarial stabilization-control review
Verify the lock notice and current counts in the rendered docs page
justifiedChanges.recommendation: Approve the bounded hardening because it directly closes demonstrated convergence bypasses at low cost and is the narrowest repair that preserves evidence-owned status progress.
